conversation_history.answer: No. Been sending out applications for weeks — nothing.
conversation_history.question: Are you currently employed?

conversation_history.answer: Yeah, I gotta work. Something's gotta pay rent. But who's hiring a 52-year-old ex-con? Doubt I'll hear back from most of them.
conversation_history.question: Are you planning to work after your release?

conversation_history.answer: Construction project management. Had my own crew before everything fell apart. That was eighteen years ago.
conversation_history.question: What kind of work were you doing before you came in?

conversation_history.answer: Anything that pays. Maybe construction again, maybe not. At my age, in my situation, you stop being picky.
conversation_history.question: What kind of work are you hoping to find after release?

conversation_history.answer: At 52? No. I know what I know. The problem's never been my skills — it's the record. A class isn't going to fix that.
conversation_history.question: Are you planning to go to school or get any training after release?

conversation_history.answer: I guess I'll have to apply for food stamps. Shouldn't need to at my age but here we are. The whole system is set up to keep you coming back.
conversation_history.question: Do you plan to apply for any financial assistance after your release?

conversation_history.answer: Same as anyone — cover rent, pay bills, eat. Nothing fancy. All these questions aren't going to change what's out there waiting for me.
conversation_history.question: Do you have any financial goals — saving for rent, building a budget?

conversation_history.answer: My ex-wife moved on long ago. My kids are grown. My daughter calls. My son less so — we're not close, and that's on me. I'm not going to pretend things are great.
conversation_history.question: Do you have family you're planning to reconnect with after release?

conversation_history.answer: Some. My daughter said I can stay in her spare room for a bit. It's not ideal for either of us but it's something.
conversation_history.question: Do you feel like you have a support system out there?

conversation_history.answer: I owned a house. It's long gone.
conversation_history.question: Where were you living before you came to this facility?

conversation_history.answer: My daughter's spare room to start. Short-term. I'll find my own place once I have income. I'm not planning to stay long.
conversation_history.question: Where will you stay right after release?

conversation_history.answer: Somewhere quiet. My own space. Not asking for much.
conversation_history.question: What are your housing goals longer-term?

conversation_history.answer: I read. I used to do woodworking — had a shop. That's gone. I'll figure something out. Maybe get back into it eventually.
conversation_history.question: What do you enjoy doing in your free time?

conversation_history.answer: My daughter mostly. Maybe a couple of old guys from my construction days, if they're still around. Most people move on after eighteen years. That's just how it is.
conversation_history.question: Who are your closest connections outside the facility?

conversation_history.answer: I drank. Not insane amounts but it contributed to some bad decisions. I'm not going to pretend otherwise.
conversation_history.question: Do you have any history with alcohol or drug use?

conversation_history.answer: Less. Maybe not at all. I'm too old for it and it's not like it ever helped.
conversation_history.question: Are you planning to drink after release?
